content.assessment: A structural grade-split reform is coalition-feasible in the near term because it can be framed as preservation rather than closure, but Hungary's own long-run history of repeated centralization/decentralization swings across regimes, and Poland's abrupt and disruptive 2017 reversal of an analogous structural reform, both indicate this proposal carries substantial reversal risk if political control changes. The current government's rural-revival narrative (Magyar Falu Program) could provide short-term legitimacy, but nothing in the evidence suggests durability across a change of governing coalition.
opportunities: Could align with the government's existing Magyar Falu rural-revival narrative, potentially securing funding and legitimacy for the retained lower-grade schools
strengths: Framing as partial preservation (retaining grades 1-4 locally) may generate broader coalition support than outright closure, since it visibly protects the youngest, most commute-vulnerable pupils
threats: Poland's 2017 reversal shows structural reforms of this kind are vulnerable to purely political reversal disconnected from evidence, with severe disruption costs (teacher displacement, unplaced cohorts) if reversed
Fiscal exposure to national class-size minimums could make even the preserved lower-grade schools targets for future closure under a different administration
weaknesses: Hungary's documented history shows rural school-network structure has swung repeatedly across political regimes, meaning a grade-split structure has no guarantee of durability independent of who governs
